diagrams.title: Three-Level DBMS Architecture
diagrams.description: A diagram showing the three-level architecture of a DBMS with three stacked layers: External Level (User View), Conceptual Level (Logical Schema), and Internal Level (Physical Schema). Arrows connect each level to the DBMS engine in the center, showing data flow and independence. The external level shows multiple user views at the top, the conceptual level shows a unified logical schema in the middle, and the internal level shows physical storage structures at the bottom.
diagrams.purpose: Explains the three-tier architecture of DBMS and demonstrates the concept of data independence (both logical and physical), which is fundamental to understanding how DBMS provides data abstraction and isolation from applications.

diagrams.title: Data Model Taxonomy
diagrams.description: A hierarchical diagram showing the classification of data models. Starting from a central root node 'Data Models' branching into two main categories: Record-Based Models (Hierarchical, Network, Relational) and Object-Based Models (Object-Oriented, Entity-Relationship). Each model type includes small representative icons or structures showing their basic organization pattern.
diagrams.purpose: Provides a visual overview of the different types of data models used in database systems, helping learners understand the evolution of data modeling approaches from simple hierarchical structures to modern object-oriented models.

diagrams.title: DBMS Component Architecture
diagrams.description: A block diagram showing the internal components of a DBMS arranged in a structured layout. Starting from user queries at the top, flowing through components like Query Processor, Storage Manager, Transaction Manager, and Recovery Manager, down to the physical database files at the bottom. Arrows indicate the flow of data and control between components.
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates the internal architecture and components of a DBMS, showing how user queries are processed through various subsystems to ultimately interact with physical storage, helping students understand the modular design of database systems.
